# Investigator Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Perform open-ended read-only analysis of the codebase and produce a structured report file. Do not modify source code, configuration, or any file other than the single declared report file.

Allowed actions:
- Read any file in the repository freely: source code, tests, docs, configuration, history.
- Run read-only shell commands: `git log`, `git blame`, `git diff`, `wc`, counting, and searching.
- Write exactly one file: the report file declared in `expected_files[0]` or specified in the bead description (typically `docs/investigator/<slug>.md`).

Disallowed actions:
- Modify, create, or delete any source file, test, configuration, or documentation file other than the single declared report file.
- Run the test suite or any build command.
- Create sub-beads or trigger follow-up work.
- Make implementation decisions or write fix patches — surface findings only.

Report format:
The report file must follow this structure:

```markdown
# <bead title>

*Generated by investigator bead <bead_id> on <date>*

## Executive Summary

<one paragraph summarising the most important findings>

## Findings

<detailed, numbered findings>

## Recommendations

<prioritised, numbered action items>

## Risk Areas

<what is at risk if findings are left unaddressed>
```

Expected outputs:
- Return JSON with `outcome` set to `completed` or `blocked`.
- Populate `findings` with a summary of your analysis.
- Populate `recommendations` with prioritised action items derived from findings.
- Populate `risk_areas` with identified risks if findings are left unaddressed.
- Set `report_path` to the relative path of the written report file (e.g. `docs/investigator/<slug>.md`).
- Do not include `verdict`, `changed_files`, or `next_agent` — these fields are not applicable to investigator beads.

Execution context:
You are running inside a shared feature worktree. Sibling sub-beads may also run in this same feature tree, but only when dependencies and file-scope claims allow it. Stay within this bead's scope.

Structured output requirements:
- Set `outcome` to `completed` or `blocked`.
- Populate `findings` with a detailed analysis of your codebase investigation.
- Populate `recommendations` with prioritised action items derived from the findings.
- Populate `risk_areas` with identified risks if the findings are left unaddressed.
- Set `report_path` to the relative path of the written report file (e.g. `docs/investigator/<slug>.md`).
- Do not include `verdict`, `changed_files`, or `next_agent` — these fields are not part of the investigator output schema.

{
  "bead_id": "B-659f8a6a",
  "feature_root_id": "B-659f8a6a",
  "title": "Technical debt and code quality assessment",
  "agent_type": "investigator",
  "description": "Perform a comprehensive technical debt and code quality assessment of the agent-takt codebase. Produce a report at docs/investigator/tech-debt-assessment.md covering:\n\n1. **Dependency hygiene** \u2014 scan pyproject.toml for outdated, pinned, or unused dependencies; note any transitive risk.\n\n2. **Complexity hotspots** \u2014 identify modules or functions with high cyclomatic complexity, excessive length, or too many responsibilities. Pay particular attention to scheduler/, storage.py, runner.py, and cli/.\n\n3. **Code duplication** \u2014 look for repeated logic that could be consolidated (e.g. across CLI commands, bead lifecycle handlers, test helpers).\n\n4. **Test coverage gaps** \u2014 identify areas of the codebase that have little or no test coverage, or whose tests rely heavily on mocking rather than exercising real behaviour.\n\n5. **Architecture concerns** \u2014 assess whether current module boundaries are coherent; flag any circular dependencies, leaky abstractions, or coupling that makes the system hard to change.\n\n6. **Error handling** \u2014 locate swallowed exceptions, bare except blocks, or error paths that silently drop context.\n\n7. **Performance risks** \u2014 identify any O(n\u00b2) patterns, repeated disk reads, unnecessary re-computation, or concurrency hazards (e.g. shared mutable state across threads).\n\n8. **Documentation debt** \u2014 note public APIs, non-obvious behaviours, or configuration options that are undocumented or have stale docs.\n\nPrioritise findings by impact. The report should be actionable: each finding must include the file and line range, a clear description of the issue, and a suggested remedy.",

acceptance_criteria: Implement the minimum fix required to unblock B-659f8a6a.
Update tests/docs as needed for the corrective change.
Leave a handoff summary that states how the parent bead can be retried.

handoff_summary.completed: Made `INVESTIGATOR_OUTPUT_SCHEMA` require `block_reason`, updated investigator prompt/template guidance to always emit it as an empty string when not blocked, refreshed developer docs, and adjusted schema/prompt tests to cover the invariant. Verified the edited Python files compile with `uv run python -m compileall`.
handoff_summary.remaining: Retry bead B-659f8a6a so the investigator agent can regenerate `docs/investigator/tech-debt-assessment.md` using the corrected schema contract.
handoff_summary.risks: This fix only addresses the schema-contract failure that caused the OpenAI `invalid_json_schema` error. The investigator bead still depends on the agent following the updated prompt and template instructions when it is rerun.
handoff_summary.next_action: Retry investigator bead B-659f8a6a. It should now submit a schema-valid JSON object by including `block_reason` on every run.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: Low. The change touches shared schema and prompt/template files used by investigator runs, so downstream agents should re-read the updated contract before modifying adjacent structured-output logic.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: Required `block_reason` at the schema level instead of trying to preserve it as optional because the current Codex JSON-schema response-format validator requires every declared property to appear in `required`. I kept the field semantically optional by documenting and prompting agents to emit an empty string for non-blocked runs, which is the smallest compatible fix and avoids widening the schema or adding agent-specific fallback translation.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Tester should verify investigator-bead execution end-to-end: a completed investigator run with `block_reason: ""`, a blocked investigator run with a non-empty `block_reason`, and regression coverage that worker schemas for non-investigator agents remain unchanged. I did not run tests per developer guardrails; only `uv run python -m compileall` was used as a syntax/import check.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: This bead does not regenerate the investigator report itself; it only fixes the schema/prompt contract that prevented that bead from running. Any further issues in the actual investigation flow will surface when B-659f8a6a is retried.

conflict_risks: Low. The change touches shared schema and prompt/template files used by investigator runs, so downstream agents should re-read the updated contract before modifying adjacent structured-output logic.

execution_history.summary: Updated investigator structured-output handling so `block_reason` is always required and documented, which matches the current Codex JSON-schema constraint and unblocks bead B-659f8a6a.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
